summary: DeVault argues that software engineers should embrace the full chain of problems that arise when fixing a bug, rather than giving up when the scope expands beyond their immediate domain. He presents a scenario where a simple bug fix escalates through third-party code, unfamiliar languages, compiler internals, and language specifications. He contends that each escalation is itself a problem that engineering skills can solve. The post reframes 'yak shaving' not as a nuisance but as a necessary and legitimate part of engineering work.
key_points: A simple bug fix can cascade into problems spanning third-party code, unfamiliar languages, compilers, and language specifications
Many engineers give up when the scope of a problem extends beyond their immediate responsibility, but this is a failure of mindset
Each layer of a problem is itself a solvable engineering problem if approached deliberately
Yak shaving is not a distraction — it's a necessary part of the engineering workflow
money_quotes: I've met many engineers who, when standing at the base of this mountain, conclude that the summit is too far away and clearly not their responsibility, and subsequently give up.
As an engineer, your job is to apply creativity to solving problems. Are these not themselves problems to which the engineering process may be applied?
The scope of fixing this bug is broader than you thought, but if you apply a deliberate engineering process to each problem that you encounter, eventually you will complete the solution.
This process of recursively solving problems to get at the one you want to solve is called "yak shaving", and it's a necessary part of your workflow.
key_insight: Engineering means recursively solving every problem that stands between you and your goal, no matter how far the chain extends beyond your original scope.
